structure.title: Large teams develop and small teams disrupt science and technology
structure.abstract: This study analyzes over 65 million papers, patents, and software products from 1954 to 2014 to demonstrate that smaller teams tend to disrupt science and technology with new ideas, while larger teams tend to develop existing ones. The findings are consistent across all fields and years.

methods.name: Disruption Index (D-index)
methods.description: A citation-based measure that quantifies the degree to which a paper disrupts or consolidates existing knowledge.

parameters.n_i: subsequent papers that cite the focal paper but not its references
parameters.n_j: papers that cite both the focal paper and its references
parameters.n_k: papers that cite only the references

analysis_steps.action: Load paper metadata and citation edges
Compute Disruption Index for each paper
Compute mean disruption score for each team-size group
analysis_steps.input: Disruption scores and team size groups
analysis_steps.output: Mean D for each team-size group
Bootstrap confidence intervals and Cohen's d effect size

evaluation.comparison_method: Bootstrap confidence intervals and field/year-level validation

structure.reproducibility_notes: The SciSciNet dataset provides the citation graph and paper metadata for reproducible computation. All analysis steps can be replicated using the dataset and Python.
